sentences.text: To define the relationship of the Jews and non-Jews in, in Hungary is not an easy task.
You would have to have a complete sociological, political, and historical exposition.
You have to understand that although in the eyes of the non-Jews, the Jews were Jews.

Still, there were very substantial differences in the relationship between Jews and non-Jews dependent on the history of the area and the nature of the Jewish and non-Jewish communities in, in the given area and era.
I come from Kisvarda, which is-- was in the very northeast of the country, [INAUDIBLE], what then was the Czech border and later on became the Russian border and the Polish border.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish community of, of, of Kisvarda was, by and large, I would say, at least one third, if not more, relatively new, recent immigrants from the east.

They were particularly the Hasidic element who were stranger than the, so to say, native Jews, who were there and belonged to the Jewish community before the days of Franz Joseph and, and what they called the 'dual monarchy.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so that is on the Jewish side so to say on the non-Jewish side you have to realize that in the area where I had lived I would say that the non-Jewish population was overwhelmingly what they called Roman Catholic.
There were some Eastern Catholics, too.
But-- and, and there were some reformed churches, too.
But the overwhelming majority of the population was-- non-Jewish population was Roman Catholic, very fundamentalist kind of Catholicism, in a very real sense, quite primitive, too.
Their clergy was almost primitive.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then, at a time-- in-- even in my days, I would say that illiteracy was probably around 60%.
It was an agrarian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] area.
In fact, our city was, so to say, the, the marketplace as well as the commercial center and the administrative center for a very large, agrarian area.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Children would be sent by their parents-- other than the professional element or the gentry, military officers, et cetera-- would go to the, the required four elementary grades or six elementary grades.
And then they would, of course, become part of the-- of the real struggle to maintain their existence.
And so the relationship of, of the Jews and the non-Jews was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made up of, of this primitive, religious, and illiterate [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] aura of, of the area.

And-- so that although that you would find that there were Jews who had relationships with non-Jews, some even quite close, certainly at the level of the thin layer of very assimilated Jews and professionals and the bankers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were pretty close relationships and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and maybe business partnerships.

But there was a wall separating the two communities that could not be penetrated and could not be broken down, even at the level of the-- of the assimilated Jews, the social [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, you know, Jews, in, in a sense, imitated the Hungarian gentry at that level.
Nonetheless, they were separate, basically.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A very interesting story of a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a kind of interesting historical vignette.
In the area there was a woman from one of the gentry families who married a, a Jew and who was invited to the annual New Year's ball, but was told to leave her husband behind.

That sort of, I would say, symbolizes that level. far as the average Hungarian and the average Jew, who usually was a bearded Jew or at least wore black clothes and certainly, the Hasidim, who wore kaftans and-- was, you know, their contact was limited to the bare essentials.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they really didn't know each other and, for that matter, looked down upon each other for their respective reasons.

And by the time of 1944, the atmosphere was, was charged with animosity that, you know, was the result of, by then, at least 20, 25 years of virulent anti-Semitism practiced by the government to the point where, at times, it was physically dangerous for the Jews or the Jews were in physical danger.
On Christian holidays, particularly when they had parades, it became very touchy.
But most of all, there was this awareness that they were they, and we were we.
And never the twain shall meet.
So it's a very-- it's a very difficult proposition.
And, you know, the history of, of the Holocaust in Hungary, or the Hungarian aspect of the Holocaust, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is marked by something very interesting, you know.
We, in the Northeast, were the dispensable Jews.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Horthy, for instance, more or less considered the Jews of Budapest and the Western part of the country who were more assimilated, more Hungarianized, less strange in their physical appearance.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He more or less considered those his Jews.
And that was one of the reasons why he halted the deportation at a certain point.
It was after-- that resumed, of course, when he fell in October 15th, I believe, of 1944.
Even though that he, he was a, a dyed-in-the-wool anti-Semite-- but he was a gentlemanly anti-Semite.
And he would not have thought of burning Jews-- or certainly had some concern for the image of Hungary in, in the world.
And so he was ready to dispense with these Northeastern Jews who were really basically the Galician Jews.
And they were not really Hungarians.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, of course, the population was aware of this attitude.
And in some ways, it was a kind of symbiotic relationship.
Because on the one hand, that was the demand of the population to which the authorities responded.
And on the other hand, the authorities have encouraged that attitude.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was, as I said, this, this wall that existed between the, the various elements of the non-Jewish community and, and the various elements of the Jewish community.
It depended on, on-- its, its nature or its character depended on the nature of the elements of the community.
If you were a Hasidic Jew, obviously, you were a stranger.
And if you were a plain peasant in Hungary, you may have even had warm feelings towards the Jews.
But you wouldn't want to get involved with them.
You wouldn't want to become, you know, not only not part of their family, but you wouldn't want to be involved in their existence and in their life, and vice versa, obviously.
You know, to, to the Jews, in a way, the non-Jews were primitive, uncouth.

And in the same fashion, you can just imagine yourself being a non-Jew and suddenly watch a Jew with his kaftan and tallis around his neck and a shtreimel on a Saturday-- what a strange apparition that was for him.
So even where there was no animus-- and there was plenty of that, certainly among the gentry.
The gentry felt itself, I, I would say, displaced by the Jews, and demanded to take the place of the Jews.
Prior to 1939, did you ever personally feel that you were in physical danger at any time?
Oh, actually, I was in physical danger as a-- as a student in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] elementary school.
At times, a, a-- our teacher would keep the few Jews that went to the government school, like myself, during the pauses that we used to have between every hour for about 10 minutes or so.
He would consider it the better part of wisdom to keep us in the classroom.
Because if we went outside, we were bound to clash with the, oh, you know, 99% of the non-Jewish students who, you know, needed really no cause to pick a fight and to bloody our noses.
Yes, it, it, it did.
Of course it did.
I hope you have several years available.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, whose name in Hungarian was Menyheart, M-E-N-Y-H-E-A-R-T, and-- but whose Hebrew and Yiddish names were Menachem Mendel, was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, a, a well-known personality in, in Hungary and beyond.

He was one of those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not rare but infrequent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lay people who insisted on remaining a layperson, but was a renowned rabbinic scholar, descendant, as he used to say, son after son after one of the-- of the great rabbis whose books are today still currently studied in the yeshivot, certainly in the Orthodox yeshivot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a rather unusual man in many ways, quite handsome, dressed in a kind of a semi-Hasidic style.
Very natty, because he was traveling, and his business was wine and liquor and beer wholesaler and exporter, above all.
The business main portion was export.
He used to have his clothes made in London.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was also a man of very big concepts, not very practical because his mind was always on his Talmudic studies.

But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I could best indicate to you how [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he conceptualized all sorts of major business moves-- that while the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Congress of the United States debated whether or not to withdraw the prohibition-- I don't remember what amendment to the Constitution it was.
I think the 22nd Amendment.
I, I, I really don't know the number.
My father already had two boatloads of wine on it-- and liquor on its way to the United States.
But, you know, he could conceptualize these major business moves.

But for execution and for management, he had to rely entirely on my mother, who not only, of course, was more at home with Hungarian and understood administration and correspondence and cables and things of that kind, but was far more practical, in, in a certain sense, than my father was.
My father was a, a kind of a very bookish man, in a way.
He was-- he had a huge library of rabbinical books and was what, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the world knows from Chaim Nahman Bialik's famous poem, he was a [INAUDIBLE].
He was a-- he could be lost for days and nights in his books.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was much sought after, particularly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as an expert in halachah, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very frequently [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called in by rabbinic courts to be an arbitrator or whatever, a dayan, or what they call a [INAUDIBLE], which, really, is kind of a counsel comme defense lawyer or something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Curiously enough, although he was a, a star student of a very famous rabbi, Hasidic rabbi, the [?

Munkacser ?] Rebbe, who was quite an institution himself, he was not really a Hasid in what came to be known in the philosophical sense-- and certainly what it came to be known in the literature of Hasidism, late Hasidism-- he was not-- he was too rational and too involved in the-- in the rationale or, or rational approach of the Talmud and rabbinic literature.
So he was not given to this-- to the mysticism [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which Hasidism absorbed and, and propagated.
He-- even his attire sort of separated him from, from Hasidism, although in his observance, he was as strict as a Hasid.
But in his ideas and, and in his, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, his interests, he was not really a Hasid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, he was very strict and very stiff-necked and above all, extremely principled.
He could tolerate no compromise when it came to-- what he could say to Judaism.
There was either his way or no way at all.
Anything that in the slightest way departed from his conception of Judaism was beyond the pale of Judaism and could not be tolerated, certainly in his own family.
And he, he would make no compromises.
He couldn't.
To him, the slightest compromise would have been tantamount to hypocrisy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, it's-- he was very intolerant in that sense.

It-- my relationship with my father was, in retrospect, I, I guess I should call it very unfortunate because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was no less devoted and dedicated to Judaism, in my perception and my conception of Judaism, and certainly in the perpetuation of Jews and Judaism, and I was as determined and as stiff-necked in my way as he was in his way.
The result was that since I was thoroughly Jewish, I was more than happy to attempt to study and meet him on the level of rabbinic studies.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But beyond that point, when it came to observance or when it came to our relationship, so to say, to the outside world, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a complete disjuncture between my father and myself.
There was absolutely no relationship.
So my relationship to with my father, I would say, could be summarized by a-- an ongoing contest between myself and him.
I wanted to demonstrate to him that I could be as knowledgeable in Judaism as he was or that he wanted me to be without being observant as he was.
Were there any activities that you participated in that he would not have sanctioned?

Well, to the extent that he was aware of it-- and when he was aware of it-- of course, that-- it was a bitter struggle between-- even from my earliest childhood on, between two stubborn, relentless wills, his and mine.
I had ceased to be observant in a real sense at the age of five.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had, in part-- and this is a completely [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] other part of the story-- in part because of my mother's influence, I become aware of the world at a very early age, and not only wanted to be part of it, but burrowed into it with the result that by the age of five, I would say, or, or certainly very shortly after that, I had rejected Hungary.

I had become, perhaps, without, you know, the formal definition, a Zionist and nationalist-- and cultural nationalist Jew, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very involved with the world, became politically conscious.
And so I literally had left the fold of my father at, at a very young age and eventually, quite naturally, tended to a more formalized Zionism to the extent that it existed in our town.

And that's another separate story of how it existed and how it didn't exist-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] became an adherent of Hashomer Hatzair-- certainly at that time and for a long time to come-- the most extreme, left-wing, socialist Zionism, and remained devoted to this-- to the essence of Hashomer Hatzair's philosophy to, to this day and probably to my dying day.
And, and became very involved with it and, and actively involved in participating in-- to the extent-- and whenever it was possible for me.
The, the conflict between my father and myself was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ceaseless and, in many ways, brutal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because as I have said, we have had two really strong wills pitted against each other uncompromisingly.
I was no less uncompromising than my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So that my relationship with my father-- and I had a great conflict about this with my sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had loved my father very deeply.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] their relationship with him was close, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even in a physical sense.
You know, girls were given much [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] looser reign.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, they didn't have to-- they didn't have to shoulder all of the burdens of Jewish observance and Jewish ritual as strictly as, as a boy had to.
And, on top of everything else, I was the only boy.
I was the kadishel.
And I had to be a, if not a, a, a clone of my father, but even more than my father, if that is possible.
And so the demands on me were much stronger and stricter and broader.
Well, it was nil.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- one of my sisters taught me how to read when I was about four years old.
And I took a, a, an exam, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] such as it was, and--
--and got into second grade.
And then went in-- to three grades.
And that was the end of my formal education.
Well, I had gone to a serious cheders and Talmud Torah and yeshivot and [?
Yeshivot ?] [?
K'Tana. ?] And eventually, I was ordained as a rabbi-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as, as I recently said to someone, the most disbelieving rabbi there ever was.
Sure.
